turns.content: I've read about embedding build metadata in Rust binaries but I still don't fully understand how to actually stamp the git SHA and version into the binary so it's available at runtime using env! or similar mechanisms.

The core idea is to write your build.rs script to execute commands like git rev-parse HEAD and cargo pkgid, then use those outputs to construct a struct that implements Serialize so you can embed it into the binary. You'll typically define a BuildInfo type with fields for commit_hash, timestamp, and version_string, derive Debug and Serialize on it, and pass an instance of this through the build process using Cargo's build dependencies.
Right, so I need to make sure my BuildInfo struct derives both Debug and Serialize? That seems like a lot of boilerplate. Can't I just store raw strings in a global variable instead?

Storing raw strings globally won't work because the binary needs to know how much space each string takes at compile time for alignment and security reasons, which only happens when you embed metadata using Serialize. You also need the struct to implement Clone so that every crate instance gets its own copy of the data without sharing mutable state across threads.
Okay, I get it now about the serialization requirement but what if my build.rs script fails partway through? Will the binary still compile even if git isn't available?

Cargo will fail to compile if build.rs panics or returns an error, so you need to handle potential failures gracefully in your build.rs. You can use Option types for fields that might be missing and provide sensible defaults like "unknown" for commit_hash when git isn't available, then return a Result<()> at the end of your script.
That makes sense but I'm still confused about how to actually call this from my main code. Do I need to manually invoke cargo metadata commands or is there an easier way?

You don't need to re-implement the logic in your main code; instead, you can use Cargo's built-in build.rs hook to generate the metadata once during compilation, then access it via env! macros or static constants defined by the build script. For example, you could define const BUILD_SHA: &str = "..." at the top of your lib.rs and reference that directly without calling any external commands.
